title: American Beef Stew
subtitle: Hearty beef and vegetable stew with potatoes, carrots, and a thick, savoury broth
sourceNotes: American beef stew is a foundational recipe of American home cooking, present in Fannie Farmer's The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896) and a fixture of American cookbooks throughout the twentieth century. The technique of thickening with flour-coated beef and potato starch is distinctly American, contrasting with the wine-based French braise.

content.text: Browning the beef properly is the flavour investment. Do it in batches, one layer at a time, high heat, without moving the beef until a crust has formed. Crowded beef steams rather than browns and produces a grey, mild-tasting broth. The
that builds up in the pan through each batch is dissolved into the broth with the stock: a significant contributor to the stew's colour and depth.

Season {{beef-chuck}} of beef and toss with {{plain-flour}} of flour. Heat the oil in a large casserole over a high heat. Brown in batches, 4 to 5 minutes per batch. Remove. Cook onion and celery in the remaining fat for 5 minutes, then add garlic and tomato purée for 1 minute. Add Worcestershire sauce and pour in {{beef-stock}} of hot beef stock, scraping the base. Return the beef, add thyme and bay leaves. Cover and simmer for 45 minutes.

Add {{waxy-potatoes}} of potato chunks and {{carrot}} of carrot pieces. Cover and cook for 45 to 55 minutes more until the beef is completely tender and the vegetables are soft. Remove the herbs, taste and adjust seasoning, scatter with parsley, and serve.
